modifications: Modified GHRH(1-29) core with four substitutions vs native GHRH: D-Ala2, Gln8, Ala15, Leu27 (confer resistance to DPP-4 cleavage and enzymatic degradation)
C-terminal lysine extension bearing a N-ε-maleimidopropionyl (MPA) group — the 'Drug Affinity Complex' (DAC) moiety
DAC forms a covalent thioether bond with Cys34 of endogenous human serum albumin in vivo, creating a long-circulating peptide-albumin conjugate
identity.structureClass: GHRH(1-29) analog with albumin-conjugating DAC linker (long-acting peptide prodrug)

classification.mechanismFamily: GHRH receptor (GHRHR) agonist (Gs/cAMP/PKA) — somatotroph-targeted GH secretagogue

tile.mechanismSummary: A tetrasubstituted, DAC-modified analog of GHRH(1-29) (sermorelin) that covalently conjugates to serum albumin after injection, producing a long-lived albumin-bound pool that chronically activates pituitary somatotroph GHRH receptors to raise GH and IGF-1 over days rather than minutes.
tile.localization: Anterior pituitary somatotrophs (GHRH receptor) → systemic GH rise → hepatic IGF-1 secretion.
clinicalUses: No approved clinical uses; was investigated for lipodystrophy and GH deficiency and reached phase 2, discontinued
Widely used off-label / in 'research-peptide' and wellness contexts for body composition and anti-aging — not supported by regulatory approval
sideEffects: Injection-site reactions, erythema, pruritus
Transient flushing, headache
Water retention, edema
Possible paresthesia
Elevations in IGF-1 that can contribute to joint discomfort, carpal tunnel symptoms, insulin resistance (extrapolated from GH class)
Theoretical oncologic risk from chronically elevated GH/IGF-1 axis (not demonstrated in short trials)

dosing.quick: Investigational / research-only. Published human phase 1/2 trials used single or weekly subcutaneous injections in the 30-250 µg/kg range. No FDA-sanctioned dosing protocol exists.

proteins: Human serum albumin (covalent carrier via Cys34)
receptors: GHRHR (class B GPCR, Gs-coupled) on anterior pituitary somatotrophs

steps: CJC-1295 DAC is injected subcutaneously and forms a covalent albumin conjugate via Michael addition of the maleimide group to albumin Cys34
Albumin-bound CJC-1295 circulates for ~6-8 days and binds GHRHR on anterior pituitary somatotrophs
Gs activation → adenylyl cyclase → cAMP rise → PKA phosphorylation → CREB-driven GH gene transcription and release
Plasma GH increases 2- to 10-fold for ~6 days after a single dose
Sustained GH elevation activates hepatic GH receptor (GHR) → JAK2/STAT5 signaling
STAT5-driven IGF1 transcription raises plasma IGF-1
In human volunteers IGF-1 rose 0.5- to 3-fold for 9-11 days per dose, and remained elevated for up to 28 days with repeated dosing

expanded.humanEvidence: Small phase 1 / phase 2 trials (Teichman et al., JCEM 2006) in healthy adults showed a single subcutaneous dose of CJC-1295 DAC produced a 2- to 10-fold increase in plasma GH sustained for up to 6 days and a 1.5- to 3-fold increase in IGF-1 sustained for 9-11 days. Multiple dosing produced IGF-1 elevations lasting up to 28 days. CJC-1295 was under investigation in lipodystrophy and adult GH deficiency but development was discontinued in the mid-2000s. One phase 2 subject died during the program; the attending physician attributed the death to asymptomatic coronary artery disease and considered it unrelated to study drug, but this event contributed to the program's halt. No completed phase 3 program, no regulatory approval in any jurisdiction.
expanded.animalEvidence: Alba et al. (Am J Physiol Endocrinol Metab 2006) showed daily, every-other-day, or every-third-day subcutaneous CJC-1295 in GHRH-knockout mice normalized body weight, length, and body composition; pituitary GH mRNA and total pituitary RNA rose, indicating somatotroph proliferation. Separate rodent PK work demonstrated the covalent albumin conjugation mechanism.
expanded.mechanismDetail: Native GHRH(1-44) has an active N-terminal 1-29 fragment (sermorelin) with a half-life of ~7 minutes, limited by DPP-4 cleavage at the Tyr1-Ala2 bond. CJC-1295 replaces that cleavage site (D-Ala2) and three other labile residues (Gln8, Ala15, Leu27) to produce a peptide that resembles the sermorelin backbone but resists enzymatic degradation. A single C-terminal lysine is then appended bearing a maleimidopropionyl (MPA) reactive group — the Drug Affinity Complex (DAC) moiety. After subcutaneous injection, the maleimide group reacts via Michael addition with the free thiol of albumin Cys34, producing a stable thioether-linked peptide-albumin conjugate that inherits albumin's ~19-day half-life. The resulting pharmacology is a long-lived, sustained GHRHR tone that amplifies endogenous GH pulses (somatostatin-driven pulsatility is preserved because the peptide only signals through GHRHR) rather than replacing them. Downstream, GH activates hepatic GHR → JAK2/STAT5 → IGF1 transcription. An unmodified variant, 'modified GRF(1-29)' / 'CJC-1295 without DAC', shares the four amino acid substitutions but lacks the MPA-lysine — its half-life is only ~30 minutes.
expanded.safetyDetail: Short-term human safety data are limited to small trials. Reported adverse events are mostly injection-site reactions, flushing, and transient water retention. One death occurred in the phase 2 program (attributed to pre-existing coronary artery disease and considered unrelated to study drug by the investigator), but it contributed to program termination. Long-term safety of sustained, non-pulsatile GH/IGF-1 elevation is unknown; theoretical risks include insulin resistance, edema, carpal tunnel syndrome, acromegalic changes, and possible promotion of occult malignancy — none of these have been rigorously characterized in CJC-1295 populations. Quality and identity of 'research-chemical' CJC-1295 sold online is not regulated; contamination and mislabeling are common concerns. WADA prohibits GHRH analogs in sport.

safetyRisks.label: Unresolved cardiovascular safety signal
safetyRisks.helpSummary: Not demonstrated to cause cardiovascular events at trial doses
safetyRisks.harmSummary: A phase 2 subject died during the CJC-1295 development program; while attributed to pre-existing coronary disease, the event contributed to program discontinuation and long-term cardiovascular safety is uncharacterized

safetyRisks.label: Chronic GH/IGF-1 elevation
safetyRisks.helpSummary: Transient GH/IGF-1 rises are part of the intended pharmacology
safetyRisks.harmSummary: Sustained non-pulsatile GH/IGF-1 tone may promote insulin resistance, fluid retention, carpal tunnel, acromegalic features, and theoretical tumor growth — not evaluated in long-term human trials

safetyRisks.label: Unregulated research-peptide supply
safetyRisks.helpSummary: Clinical-grade material was produced for trials and is no longer available
safetyRisks.harmSummary: Current access is almost entirely via unregulated 'research-use-only' vendors with no guaranteed purity, sterility, or identity; banned by WADA

enhancementPotential.summary: Small trials documented meaningful GH/IGF-1 elevation, but there are no controlled data in healthy non-deficient adults showing durable body-composition, strength, cognitive, or longevity benefits.
enhancementPotential.caveat: Off-label use in healthy adults is not supported by any approved indication or long-term outcome data. Extrapolating 'more GH/IGF-1 is better' from small endocrine studies is not warranted.

missingEvidence: Long-term (>1 year) safety data in any human population
Completed phase 3 efficacy trials for any indication
Cardiovascular and oncologic outcome data
Human data in healthy adults seeking body-composition benefits (only short, small studies exist)
anecdotalUse: Widely used off-label in anti-aging and bodybuilding contexts, often paired with a GHRP (e.g., ipamorelin) to stack GHRH-driven GH release with ghrelin-receptor-mediated somatostatin suppression

claims.value: Not approved in any jurisdiction; clinical development was discontinued after phase 2
claims.population: healthy adults and patients with lipodystrophy / GH deficiency

claims.value: DAC-mediated covalent albumin conjugation extends half-life to ~6-8 days in humans; a single subcutaneous dose raises GH 2- to 10-fold for up to 6 days and IGF-1 0.5- to 3-fold for 9-11 days; with repeated dosing IGF-1 remains elevated up to 28 days

Tetrasubstituted (D-Ala2/Gln8/Ala15/Leu27) GHRH(1-29) analog with C-terminal Lys-MPA 'DAC' that covalently binds albumin Cys34; acts as a sustained GHRHR agonist driving Gs/cAMP signaling in anterior pituitary somatotrophs to amplify endogenous GH release
claims.population: GHRHKO mice and in vitro receptor pharmacology

citations.title: CJC-1295 — Wikipedia
citations.notes: Summarizes tetrasubstitution, DAC chemistry, PK (6-8 day half-life; GH/IGF-1 duration), and trial discontinuation after a subject death attributed to pre-existing coronary disease.

citations.title: Prolonged stimulation of growth hormone (GH) and insulin-like growth factor I secretion by CJC-1295, a long-acting analog of GH-releasing hormone, in healthy adults (Teichman SL, Neale A, Lawrence B, Gagnon C, Castaigne J-P, Frohman LA; JCEM 2006;91(3):799-805)
citations.notes: Primary human PK/PD study documenting GH and IGF-1 elevation duration.

citations.title: Once-daily administration of CJC-1295, a long-acting growth hormone-releasing hormone (GHRH) analog, normalizes growth in the GHRH knockout mouse (Alba M, Fintini D, Sagazio A, Lawrence B, Castaigne J-P, Frohman LA, Salvatori R; AJP Endocrinol Metab 2006;291:E1290-1294)
citations.notes: Demonstrates CJC-1295 normalizes growth in GHRH-KO mice and drives somatotroph proliferation.

citations.title: What is CJC-1295 without DAC? (BOC Sciences)
citations.notes: Vendor comparison of CJC-1295 (DAC) vs modified GRF(1-29); confirms half-life differences.

modifications: Based on the 70-residue human IGF-1 backbone
Substitution of Glu3 with Arg — the 'R3' modification — which dramatically reduces affinity for IGF-binding proteins (IGFBPs)
Three intramolecular disulfide bonds retained from native IGF-1
identity.structureClass: recombinant IGF-1 analog (extended, arginine-3 substituted; IGF-1R agonist with reduced IGFBP affinity)

classification.mechanismFamily: IGF-1 receptor (IGF-1R) agonist (receptor tyrosine kinase → PI3K/AKT/mTOR and MAPK/ERK)

tile.mechanismSummary: Synthetic, recombinant IGF-1 analog engineered with an N-terminal 13-amino-acid extension and a Glu3→Arg substitution so it retains full IGF-1 receptor agonism but binds IGF-binding proteins very weakly, keeping more peptide free and bioactive and extending plasma half-life to ~20-30 hours.
tile.localization: IGF-1R-expressing tissues — especially skeletal muscle, liver, bone, neurons, cartilage.
clinicalUses: No approved human therapeutic use. Native recombinant IGF-1 (mecasermin / Increlex) is FDA-approved for severe primary IGF-1 deficiency; IGF-1 LR3 is not approved for any indication.
Used as a research reagent (e.g., in cell culture media) and widely used off-label in athletic / 'research peptide' contexts for anabolic purposes — not supported by regulatory approval.
sideEffects: Hypoglycemia (especially at higher doses or when combined with insulin)
Muscle and joint pain
Headache
Nausea
Injection-site reactions
Theoretical promotion of occult malignancy via IGF-1R activation (not demonstrated in short-term human data)
Prohibited in competitive sport (WADA)

dosing.quick: Research-only; no approved human dosing protocol exists. Community 'research peptide' protocols commonly cite 20-100 µg subcutaneous once daily, but these are not clinically validated.

steps: IGF-1 LR3 binds IGF-1R on the cell surface
Autophosphorylation of the IGF-1R β-subunit tyrosine kinase domain
Recruitment of IRS-1/IRS-2 adaptors and Shc
PI3K activation → AKT phosphorylation → mTORC1 activation → increased protein synthesis and cellular hypertrophy; AKT-mediated inhibition of FoxO and GSK3β reduces protein breakdown
Parallel MAPK/ERK1/2 activation drives satellite cell proliferation and myoblast differentiation
Glu3→Arg substitution plus the 13-aa N-terminal extension destabilize the IGFBP-binding interface
IGFBPs (especially IGFBP-3) retain free native IGF-1 in circulation; IGF-1 LR3 largely escapes this pool
A higher fraction of IGF-1 LR3 remains receptor-available, yielding greater bioactivity per molecule and a ~20-30 h plasma half-life vs ~10-20 min for native IGF-1

expanded.humanEvidence: No controlled human trials support IGF-1 LR3 as a therapeutic. Native IGF-1 (mecasermin / Increlex) has FDA approval for severe primary IGF-1 deficiency; that approval does not extend to IGF-1 LR3. Short-term intravenous or subcutaneous IGF-1 (native) in humans produces hypoglycemia and suppresses GH. Trials using the closely related LR3 analog in humans are essentially absent — most human data are extrapolations from native IGF-1 pharmacology and from preclinical IGF-1 LR3 studies in rodents and cell models.
expanded.animalEvidence: Extensive preclinical work shows IGF-1 LR3 is ~3× more potent than native IGF-1 in muscle cell proliferation and protein-synthesis assays, reflecting its reduced IGFBP affinity and longer half-life. Rodent studies confirm anabolic effects on skeletal muscle, stimulation of satellite cell proliferation, and pro-differentiation effects on myoblasts. IGF-1 LR3 is routinely used as a cell-culture reagent in stem cell, tissue engineering, and vaccine manufacturing.
expanded.mechanismDetail: Native IGF-1 is a 70-amino-acid peptide with three intramolecular disulfide bonds and a plasma half-life of ~10-20 minutes, constrained by rapid binding to IGFBPs (especially IGFBP-3) that form a ternary complex with the acid-labile subunit. IGF-1 LR3 adds 13 amino acids (MFPAMPLLSLFVN) at the N-terminus and swaps Glu3 for Arg, producing an 83-residue analog. These modifications preserve full agonism at IGF-1R but reduce IGFBP affinity by several orders of magnitude, so a larger fraction of circulating LR3 is free and receptor-available. Half-life extends to ~20-30 hours. Relative to insulin receptor engagement, IGF-1 LR3 also shows ~10-fold lower IR cross-reactivity than native IGF-1, favoring IGF-1R-specific anabolic signaling over insulin-like glucose disposal. Downstream, IGF-1R activation drives PI3K/AKT/mTORC1 (protein synthesis, anti-proteolysis, satellite cell survival) and MAPK/ERK1/2 (satellite cell proliferation, myoblast differentiation) cascades. At concentrations above ~500 nM, hybrid IGF-1R/IR receptor engagement becomes significant and introduces insulin-like glucose uptake effects.
expanded.safetyDetail: Acute safety concerns reflect the IGF-1 class: hypoglycemia (more likely with rapid-acting or high doses, or with concomitant insulin), injection-site reactions, myalgia/arthralgia, and headache. Long-term safety of supraphysiologic IGF-1R stimulation in healthy adults is not established; IGF-1R activation is implicated in the proliferation of multiple cancer types, so chronic off-label use raises theoretical oncologic concern. There are case reports of central serous chorioretinopathy attributed to high-dose off-label IGF-1 analog use. IGF-1 LR3 is banned in sport by WADA. 'Research-peptide' supply is unregulated; purity, identity, and sterility are not guaranteed.

safetyRisks.label: Hypoglycemia
safetyRisks.helpSummary: Controlled research dosing minimizes this risk
safetyRisks.harmSummary: Potent IGF-1R agonism and low-affinity insulin-receptor crossover can drop blood glucose, especially with high doses or insulin co-use

safetyRisks.label: IGF-1R stimulation and cancer risk
safetyRisks.helpSummary: Short-term use in research settings has not demonstrated tumor development
safetyRisks.harmSummary: Chronic supraphysiologic IGF-1R activation is mechanistically linked to proliferation of several tumor types; long-term safety in healthy users is uncharacterized

safetyRisks.label: Central serous chorioretinopathy (case reports)
safetyRisks.harmSummary: High-dose off-label IGF-1/IGF-1 LR3 use has been associated with CSC-like vision changes in case reports

safetyRisks.label: Research-peptide supply, sports-ban
safetyRisks.helpSummary: Clinical-grade IGF-1 (mecasermin) is available under regulated prescription
safetyRisks.harmSummary: IGF-1 LR3 sold for 'research use only' lacks regulatory oversight; prohibited in competitive sport by WADA

enhancementPotential.summary: Preclinical data support anabolic and satellite-cell effects in muscle. There are no controlled human trials demonstrating durable, safe enhancement of body composition or performance in healthy adults.
enhancementPotential.caveat: Extrapolation from rodent / cell-culture data to healthy humans is speculative. The best-studied IGF-1 therapeutic (mecasermin) is reserved for severe IGF-1 deficiency because of hypoglycemia and other safety constraints even in severely deficient patients.

missingEvidence: Randomized controlled human trials of IGF-1 LR3 for any indication
Long-term safety and oncologic outcome data
Pharmacokinetic / pharmacodynamic studies in healthy adults
Dose-response data in aging or sarcopenia populations
anecdotalUse: Used off-label in bodybuilding contexts for claimed muscle hypertrophy and recovery; often stacked with GH secretagogues
Used as a cell-culture reagent in academic and biomanufacturing research

claims.value: Not approved by any regulator for human therapeutic use; research-reagent grade only. Native IGF-1 (mecasermin/Increlex) is FDA-approved for severe primary IGF-1 deficiency but that approval does not extend to the LR3 analog

83-residue recombinant IGF-1 analog (13-aa N-terminal extension + Glu3→Arg) that retains full IGF-1R agonism while binding IGFBPs weakly; ~3× more potent than native IGF-1 with ~20-30 h half-life vs ~10-20 min
claims.population: in vitro receptor and cell assays; rodent PK studies

claims.value: IGF-1R activation → PI3K/AKT/mTORC1 (protein synthesis, anti-proteolysis) and MAPK/ERK (satellite cell proliferation, myoblast differentiation)

modifications: 344-residue precursor isoform of follistatin generated by alternative splicing of the FST gene; post-translationally processed to the mature ~315-residue serum form (FS-315)
Three cysteine-rich follistatin domains (FSD1, FSD2, FSD3) each stabilized by 10 conserved disulfide bonds
N-terminal domain mediates type I TGF-β-family ligand binding (myostatin, activin, GDF-11, select BMPs)
Alternative splice isoforms: FS-288 (membrane/heparan-sulfate-binding, 288-aa), FS-315 (serum, 315-aa); FS-344 is the 344-aa precursor that is cleaved to FS-315
identity.structureClass: glycoprotein of the follistatin family; TGF-β superfamily ligand trap (activin / myostatin / GDF-11 inhibitor)

classification.mechanismFamily: extracellular antagonist of myostatin (GDF-8), activins A/B, GDF-11, and select BMPs — prevents ligand binding to ActRIIB / ALK receptors

tile.mechanismSummary: Secreted glycoprotein that binds and neutralizes multiple TGF-β superfamily ligands (most notably myostatin/GDF-8 and activin A/B), preventing their engagement of ActRIIB / ALK4/5 and thereby lifting the SMAD2/3-mediated brake on skeletal muscle growth.
tile.localization: Extracellular space of skeletal muscle and most tissues; FS-315 circulates in serum, FS-288 binds heparan sulfate proteoglycans on cell surfaces.
clinicalUses: Investigated as AAV1-delivered FS344 gene therapy for Becker muscular dystrophy (Mendell/Al-Zaidy et al., phase 1/2a); not approved
Preclinical investigation in Duchenne muscular dystrophy, sarcopenia, inclusion body myositis, and cachexia models
sideEffects: Case report of central serous chorioretinopathy with high-dose off-label injectable follistatin
Theoretical reproductive endocrine effects via activin binding → altered FSH
Off-target effects on BMP signaling (bone remodeling, vasculature) with systemic exposure
Immunogenicity concerns with repeated exogenous protein administration
AAV gene therapy carries vector-specific risks (immune response, liver enzyme elevation)
Prohibited in sport by WADA (Section S4.4, myostatin modulators)

dosing.quick: Investigational. Human exposure is essentially limited to the AAV1-FS344 gene therapy phase 1/2a trial in Becker muscular dystrophy, which used intramuscular AAV1 injection to specific muscles; there is no validated injectable-peptide dosing protocol in humans.
dosing.adminRoute: intramuscular AAV gene therapy (trial); exogenous protein injection has no validated human dosing

receptors: Follistatin has no intrinsic receptor; it sequesters ligands that otherwise bind ActRIIB-ALK4/5 complexes on muscle cells

cytokinesInterleukins.name: TGF-β superfamily: myostatin (GDF-8)
cytokinesInterleukins.context: prevents SMAD2/3-driven catabolic gene expression in muscle

cytokinesInterleukins.name: Activin A / Activin B
cytokinesInterleukins.effect: high-affinity sequestration by follistatin (K_d ~50-100 pM for activin A)
cytokinesInterleukins.context: activin binding is essentially irreversible; complex cleared via receptor-mediated endocytosis

cytokinesInterleukins.name: GDF-11 and select BMPs (BMP-2, -4, -6, -7)
cytokinesInterleukins.effect: bound with lower affinity than activin/myostatin
cytokinesInterleukins.context: affects bone remodeling and neural development — basis for BMP-related safety considerations

steps: Normally: myostatin (GDF-8) binds ActRIIB on skeletal muscle, recruits ALK4/5, phosphorylates SMAD2/3, promotes atrophic gene programs and suppresses protein synthesis
Follistatin binds myostatin extracellularly and blocks ActRIIB engagement
SMAD2/3 signaling is silenced; satellite cell proliferation and muscle fiber hypertrophy proceed without the usual myostatin brake
Follistatin also sequesters activin A/B and GDF-11, removing additional TGF-β family negative regulators of muscle and tissue growth

Transgenic overexpression of follistatin produces 194-327% increase in mouse muscle mass — larger than the myostatin-KO phenotype — because follistatin neutralizes multiple ligands, not myostatin alone
Activin B stimulates pituitary FSH secretion
Follistatin isoforms bind activin and reduce pituitary activin availability
FS-344/FS-315 has ~10-fold lower affinity for activin than FS-288, which is why FS-344 was selected for gene therapy — to minimize systemic FSH suppression and reproductive side effects

expanded.humanEvidence: Limited. The best-documented human exposure is a phase 1/2a trial of intramuscular AAV1-FS344 gene therapy in Becker muscular dystrophy (Mendell, Al-Zaidy et al.), which reported improvements in 6-minute walk distance and quadriceps strength and was generally well tolerated. Systemic follistatin levels remained below thresholds that alter FSH or activin signaling, consistent with primarily local muscle expression. No randomized, placebo-controlled trials of exogenous follistatin-344 protein injection exist in humans; almost all 'peptide' injection data are uncontrolled, anecdotal, or extrapolated from gene therapy.
expanded.animalEvidence: Transgenic mice expressing high follistatin show 194-327% muscle mass increases (Lee & McPherron 2001) — greater than myostatin-KO — reflecting multi-ligand neutralization. Single intramuscular AAV1-FS344 injection in mdx mice produced muscle hypertrophy and improved strength sustained for >2 years (Haidet et al., PNAS 2008). Nonhuman primate (cynomolgus macaque) studies demonstrated ~15-20% increases in muscle size from AAV-delivered follistatin, validating the gene therapy approach for translation.
expanded.mechanismDetail: The FST gene produces two primary splice isoforms: FS-344, which is post-translationally processed into the mature 315-residue circulating form (FS-315), and FS-317, which yields the membrane-associated 288-residue form (FS-288). FS-288 has ~10-fold higher affinity for activin and binds heparan sulfate proteoglycans strongly, localizing to ovarian follicles and the pituitary — where it potently suppresses FSH. FS-344/FS-315 circulates in serum, has lower activin affinity, and is the preferred isoform for therapeutic development because it produces muscle-directed effects with substantially less pituitary-FSH suppression. All isoforms share three cysteine-rich follistatin domains and act extracellularly as high-affinity traps for myostatin, activin A/B, GDF-11, and (with lower affinity) several BMPs. The follistatin-activin complex is essentially irreversible (K_d ~50-100 pM) and is cleared via receptor-mediated endocytosis. By sequestering these ligands, follistatin prevents engagement of ActRIIB-ALK4/5 receptor complexes and silences SMAD2/3-driven atrophic gene programs in muscle, releasing satellite cell proliferation and myofiber hypertrophy.
expanded.safetyDetail: Gene therapy (AAV1-FS344) was generally well tolerated in the Becker muscular dystrophy phase 1/2a, with no significant reproductive endocrine changes detected. Safety of exogenous protein injection in healthy adults is essentially uncharacterized. Theoretical concerns: (1) reproductive endocrine effects (activin binding → reduced FSH, potential menstrual or spermatogenic changes) — mitigated but not eliminated by the FS-344/FS-315 isoform's lower activin affinity; (2) off-target BMP antagonism (bone remodeling, angiogenesis); (3) immunogenicity with repeated dosing of exogenous protein; (4) rapid serum clearance of injected follistatin (half-life ~90 minutes) means subcutaneous injections likely produce much weaker sustained effects than gene-therapy expression. A case report describes central serous chorioretinopathy in an individual using a high-dose injectable follistatin. Follistatin is prohibited in sport by WADA.

safetyRisks.label: Activin binding → FSH suppression
safetyRisks.helpSummary: FS-344/FS-315 has lower activin affinity than FS-288; gene therapy data show minimal systemic FSH change
safetyRisks.harmSummary: Systemic high-dose exposure could still suppress FSH and disrupt fertility; reproductive endpoints are not systematically monitored in off-label use

safetyRisks.label: BMP antagonism
safetyRisks.helpSummary: Clinically silent in short-term gene therapy trials
safetyRisks.harmSummary: Follistatin also binds BMPs that regulate osteogenesis and vascular integrity; chronic systemic use could plausibly affect bone or vessel biology

safetyRisks.label: Central serous chorioretinopathy (case report)
safetyRisks.harmSummary: A case report describes CSC with high-dose (~1 mg) single injection of follistatin peptide, 10× typical community protocol

safetyRisks.label: Short peptide half-life, unregulated supply
safetyRisks.helpSummary: Gene therapy provides sustained local expression
safetyRisks.harmSummary: Injected follistatin protein has ~90-minute half-life, so daily injections are unlikely to reproduce gene-therapy effects; 'research-use-only' products lack quality control; WADA-banned

enhancementPotential.summary: Strong myostatin biology and compelling gene-therapy data in animals and in one BMD trial. Evidence for exogenous follistatin-344 peptide injection producing meaningful muscle gains in healthy humans is essentially absent.
enhancementPotential.caveat: Gene therapy (sustained, local expression) is fundamentally different pharmacology from a transient subcutaneous peptide injection. Extrapolating gene-therapy muscle effects to 'research peptide' injections in healthy adults is not supported.

missingEvidence: Randomized controlled human trials of exogenous follistatin-344 protein injection
Dose-response data in healthy adults
Long-term reproductive, bone, and vascular safety outcomes
Pharmacokinetic characterization of subcutaneous follistatin in humans
anecdotalUse: Used off-label in bodybuilding / 'research peptide' circles at 100-200 µg/day SC for muscle hypertrophy — not supported by controlled human data
AAV-FS344 remains an active research program for muscular dystrophy and sarcopenia

claims.value: Investigational. One completed phase 1/2a AAV1-FS344 gene therapy trial in Becker muscular dystrophy; no approved therapeutic use anywhere; prohibited in competitive sport under WADA S4.4

344-aa precursor isoform processed to serum FS-315; selected over FS-288 because of ~10-fold lower activin affinity; sequesters myostatin (GDF-8), activins A/B, GDF-11, and certain BMPs to block ActRIIB-SMAD2/3 signaling in muscle
claims.population: transgenic mice, mdx mice, nonhuman primates, in vitro receptor assays

claims.value: Transgenic follistatin mice develop 194-327% muscle mass increases via combined hyperplasia and hypertrophy; single AAV1-FS344 IM injection sustains muscle hypertrophy and improved function in mdx mice for >2 years; nonhuman primate studies show ~15-20% muscle size increases
claims.population: transgenic mice, mdx mice, cynomolgus macaques

modifications: Encoded by KISS1 (chromosome 1q32) as a 145-residue prepro-kisspeptin
Proteolytic processing generates KP54 (the principal circulating form) and the shorter KP14, KP13, and KP10
All active kisspeptins share an amidated C-terminal Arg-Phe-NH2 (RF-NH2) motif essential for KISS1R binding
Short plasma half-life in humans (~4 minutes for KP10; tens of minutes for KP54)
identity.structureClass: RFamide neuropeptide family (KP54 main product; KP10 minimal receptor-active fragment)

classification.mechanismFamily: KISS1R (GPR54) agonist — Gq/11 → PLC → IP3/Ca2+/DAG-PKC → ERK1/2, p38

tile.mechanismSummary: Endogenous hypothalamic neuropeptide family encoded by KISS1 that acts on the GPCR KISS1R (GPR54) on GnRH neurons to trigger pulsatile GnRH release — the master upstream switch of puberty, the reproductive axis, and the mid-cycle LH surge.
tile.localization: Hypothalamic KNDy neurons (arcuate nucleus, AVPV/preoptic area) → GnRH neuron cell bodies and terminals in the median eminence → anterior pituitary gonadotrophs.
clinicalUses: Investigational for IVF oocyte maturation / ovulation trigger (KP54, reduced OHSS risk)
Investigational for hypothalamic amenorrhea and PCOS reproductive dysfunction
Diagnostic probe of GnRH neuron function (kisspeptin challenge test)
Investigational for hypoactive sexual desire disorder (HSDD) based on Imperial College London trials
sideEffects: Transient headache
Mild nausea (at higher infusion rates)
Injection-site discomfort
Theoretical reproductive/endocrine effects of chronic administration (tachyphylaxis with sustained high exposure)
Overall safety profile in completed human trials has been favorable

dosing.quick: Investigational only. Published human regimens include: IVF trigger (KP54) single SC injection ~3.2-12.8 nmol/kg; hypothalamic amenorrhea (KP54) 0.01-1.00 nmol/kg/hr IV infusion; HSDD studies (KP54) 1 nmol/kg/hr IV for ~75 min; KP10 ~1 µg/kg IV bolus for physiological probing.

proteins: Neurokinin B, Dynorphin (co-expressed by KNDy neurons)
receptors: KISS1R / GPR54 (7-transmembrane Gq/11-coupled GPCR expressed on GnRH neurons and in peripheral tissues)

steps: Hypothalamic KNDy neurons (arcuate nucleus) release kisspeptin onto GnRH neurons
Kisspeptin binds KISS1R on GnRH neurons → Gq/11 → PLCβ → PIP2 → IP3 + DAG
IP3 releases intracellular Ca2+; DAG activates PKC; downstream MAPK (ERK1/2) and p38 phosphorylation sustain depolarization
GnRH neuron firing increases → pulsatile GnRH release into hypophyseal portal circulation
Anterior pituitary gonadotrophs release LH and FSH → gonadal sex steroid production and gametogenesis
Arcuate KNDy neurons mediate sex-steroid negative feedback on GnRH pulsatility
AVPV / preoptic kisspeptin neurons mediate positive feedback driving the preovulatory LH surge in females
Dysregulation of this system underlies hypogonadotropic hypogonadism (KISS1 / KISS1R loss-of-function) and central precocious puberty (gain-of-function)
Kisspeptin signaling extends beyond the hypothalamus into limbic structures implicated in sexual arousal and bonding
Imperial College studies (Thurston/Mills/Dhillo) show KP54 infusion enhances sexual brain processing and penile tumescence/subjective desire in HSDD

expanded.humanEvidence: Extensive human physiological and phase 1/2 trial data. (1) Pediatric and adult genetic studies: loss-of-function KISS1R mutations cause congenital GnRH deficiency and failure to enter puberty; gain-of-function mutations cause central precocious puberty (Seminara et al., de Roux et al., NEJM/PNAS 2003). (2) Reproductive endocrinology: KP54 and KP10 infusion robustly raise LH/FSH in healthy adults; KP54 has been used successfully as an IVF oocyte-maturation trigger with a favorable ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome (OHSS) profile. (3) Hypothalamic amenorrhea and PCOS: KP54 partially restores pulsatile LH in hypothalamic amenorrhea; analogs like TAK-448 and MVT-602 have shown agonist activity in healthy women and women with PCOS/HA. (4) Sexual desire disorders: two double-blind crossover trials at Imperial College London (Thurston et al., JAMA Network Open 2022, n=32 women with HSDD; Mills et al., JAMA Network Open 2023, n=32 men with HSDD) showed KP54 infusion modulated sexual brain processing (fMRI) and — in men — increased penile tumescence by up to 56% vs placebo.
expanded.animalEvidence: Foundational rodent, sheep, and primate studies established kisspeptin/KISS1R as the upstream trigger of GnRH pulsatility and puberty. KISS1 or KISS1R knockout in mice and rats recapitulates the human iHH phenotype (infertility, pubertal failure). Kisspeptin-induced puberty acceleration in rats and monkeys confirms causal hierarchy.
expanded.mechanismDetail: Kisspeptin is encoded by the KISS1 gene as a 145-residue prepro-kisspeptin, which is proteolytically processed to KP54 (the main circulating form; sometimes called metastin for its original identification as a melanoma metastasis suppressor) and the shorter KP14, KP13, and KP10 fragments. All four share the amidated C-terminal Arg-Phe-NH2 (RF-NH2) motif required for high-affinity KISS1R binding; KP10 is the minimal active sequence. KISS1R is a class A Gq/11-coupled GPCR on chromosome 19p13.3 expressed on hypothalamic GnRH neurons and in several peripheral tissues. Activation drives PLCβ → PIP2 hydrolysis → IP3-triggered Ca2+ release and DAG/PKC activation, with downstream ERK1/2 and p38 MAPK phosphorylation. Kisspeptin neurons of the arcuate nucleus co-express neurokinin B and dynorphin (KNDy neurons), and these three peptides together shape pulsatile GnRH output; AVPV/preoptic kisspeptin neurons drive the positive-feedback LH surge. Circulating kisspeptin half-life is short (~4 min for KP10, longer for KP54), motivating longer-acting agonist analogs like MVT-602, TAK-448, TAK-683, and FTM080.
expanded.safetyDetail: Across published human studies (IVF trigger, hypothalamic amenorrhea, HSDD, healthy volunteers), kisspeptin and its agonists have been well tolerated with no serious drug-related adverse events reported. Transient headache, mild nausea, and injection-site discomfort are the most common events. As an IVF trigger, KP54 appears to reduce OHSS risk relative to hCG. Long-term safety data and effects of chronic receptor activation are limited. Tachyphylaxis can develop with sustained high-rate infusion. MVT-602 is a longer-acting KISS1R agonist with more prolonged LH/FSH elevation; it has been studied in PCOS and hypothalamic amenorrhea by Abbara, Dhillo and colleagues. No approved therapeutic indication in any jurisdiction as of April 2026.

safetyRisks.label: Altered gonadotropin dynamics
safetyRisks.helpSummary: Pulsatile, context-appropriate kisspeptin signaling is physiological
safetyRisks.harmSummary: Chronic or supraphysiologic KISS1R agonism could drive tachyphylaxis and unwanted menstrual/endocrine changes; uncharacterized long-term data

safetyRisks.label: Transient headache, nausea, flushing
safetyRisks.helpSummary: Typically mild and self-limited at trial doses
safetyRisks.harmSummary: Minor adverse events reported in several infusion studies

enhancementPotential.summary: Directly relevant to low-libido (HSDD) and fertility-support applications, with promising early clinical signals. Not studied for generic sexual or endocrine 'enhancement' in healthy adults.
enhancementPotential.caveat: Published benefits in HSDD and IVF are from supervised infusions of pharmaceutical-grade KP54 under IRB-approved protocols — not subcutaneous self-administration of research-chemical kisspeptin, which has no human dose-response data.

missingEvidence: Phase 3 trials for any reproductive or sexual indication
Chronic/long-term safety of KISS1R agonists
Data in post-menopausal populations
Head-to-head comparisons of KP10, KP54, TAK-448, and MVT-602
anecdotalUse: Sold in gray-market 'research peptide' channels with marketing around libido and testosterone; clinical-grade evidence supporting self-administration is absent

claims.value: Encoded by KISS1; the 145-aa prepro-kisspeptin is processed to KP54 (main form), KP14, KP13, and KP10, all sharing the amidated RF-NH2 C-terminal motif required for KISS1R binding
Kisspeptin binds KISS1R/GPR54 (Gq/11-coupled) on hypothalamic GnRH neurons, activating PLCβ → IP3/Ca2+/DAG-PKC → ERK1/2, p38 MAPK; drives pulsatile GnRH release and downstream LH/FSH secretion
Human loss-of-function mutations in KISS1 or KISS1R cause congenital GnRH deficiency and failure of pubertal initiation; gain-of-function mutations cause central precocious puberty
claims.population: consanguineous families and index cases with iHH or CPP

claims.value: In two double-blind crossover RCTs (Imperial College London), KP54 IV infusion modulated sexual brain processing vs placebo in HSDD populations; in men, KP54 increased penile tumescence by up to 56% vs placebo and improved behavioral measures of sexual desire
claims.population: premenopausal women (n=32) and heterosexual men (n=32) with HSDD

citations.title: Advances in clinical applications of kisspeptin-GnRH pathway in female reproduction (Reproductive Biology and Endocrinology, 2022)
citations.notes: Summarizes KISS1 gene product processing, KISS1R signaling (Gq/11, PLC, ERK/p38), and clinical applications.

citations.notes: Detailed review of KNDy neuron biology, KISS1R chromosomal location, and reproductive axis function.

citations.title: Inactivating mutations of GPR54 and idiopathic hypogonadotropic hypogonadism (Seminara SB et al., NEJM 2003; de Roux N et al., PNAS 2003)
citations.notes: Parallel 2003 reports establishing KISS1R loss of function as a cause of congenital GnRH deficiency in humans.

citations.title: Effects of Kisspeptin Administration in Women With Hypoactive Sexual Desire Disorder: A Randomized Clinical Trial (Thurston L et al., JAMA Network Open 2022)
citations.notes: Double-blind crossover placebo-controlled trial of KP54 in 32 premenopausal women with HSDD.

citations.title: Effects of Kisspeptin on Sexual Brain Processing and Penile Tumescence in Men With Hypoactive Sexual Desire Disorder: A Randomized Clinical Trial (Mills EG et al., JAMA Network Open 2023)
citations.notes: Men with HSDD; KP54 increased penile tumescence up to 56% vs placebo and modulated sexual brain processing on fMRI.

citations.title: Kisspeptin receptor agonist has therapeutic potential for female reproductive disorders (Abbara A et al., J Clin Invest 2020)
citations.notes: MVT-602 KISS1R agonist trial in healthy women, PCOS, and hypothalamic amenorrhea — longer LH response than KP54.

citations.title: Kisspeptin — Wikipedia
citations.notes: Summary of KISS1 gene, KP54/14/13/10 isoforms, and RF-NH2 motif.

identity.sequenceThreeLetter: H-Cys-Tyr-Ile-Gln-Asn-Cys-Pro-Leu-Gly-NH2 (disulfide bridge between Cys1 and Cys6)
modifications: Cyclic nonapeptide stabilized by a single intramolecular disulfide bond between Cys1 and Cys6 (forms a 6-residue ring with a 3-residue tail)
C-terminal glycine amidation (Gly-NH2), catalyzed in vivo by peptidylglycine α-amidating monooxygenase (PAM; ascorbate-dependent)
Differs from vasopressin by only two residues (Ile→Phe at position 3 and Leu→Arg at position 8)
identity.structureClass: cyclic (disulfide-bridged) nonapeptide hormone; neurohypophysial hormone family

classification.mechanismFamily: oxytocin receptor (OXTR) agonist — Gq-coupled GPCR → PLC → IP3/Ca2+/DAG; uterotonic and neuromodulatory

tile.mechanismSummary: Endogenous nonapeptide hormone/neurotransmitter synthesized in hypothalamic paraventricular and supraoptic nuclei and released from the posterior pituitary. Binds the Gq-coupled OXTR to trigger PLC/IP3/Ca2+ signaling — peripherally driving uterine contraction and milk letdown, and centrally modulating social bonding, trust, anxiety, and stress circuits.
tile.localization: Uterine myometrium, mammary myoepithelium (peripheral); amygdala, hypothalamus, nucleus accumbens, prefrontal cortex, brainstem (central).
clinicalUses: FDA-approved (Pitocin) for induction of labor when medically indicated
FDA-approved for augmentation of dysfunctional labor
FDA-approved for control of postpartum hemorrhage / uterine atony after placental delivery
Historically used intranasally (Syntocinon) to support milk letdown (now discontinued in the US)

Off-label / investigational: autism spectrum disorder social cognition, PTSD, social anxiety, schizophrenia negative symptoms, frontotemporal dementia (phase 2), couple therapy adjunct — none FDA-approved
sideEffects: IV/IM Pitocin: uterine hyperstimulation (tachysystole), fetal distress, postpartum hemorrhage if misused, water intoxication / hyponatremia (antidiuretic effect at high doses), nausea, vomiting, hypertension or hypotension, arrhythmias
Intranasal (off-label): nasal irritation, mild drowsiness, headache
Potential receptor desensitization with frequent dosing
Contraindications for IV/IM use: fetal distress, cephalopelvic disproportion, prior uterine surgery in some scenarios, placenta previa

dosing.quick: FDA-approved labor induction: IV infusion starting at 0.5-1 mU/min, titrated to uterine response (typical max 20 mU/min). Postpartum hemorrhage: 10-40 units in 1 L crystalloid IV, or 10 units IM after placental delivery. Intranasal 'social / wellness' oxytocin is NOT FDA-approved; research protocols commonly use 24 IU intranasal.

proteins: Oxytocin receptor (OXTR, 389-aa class A GPCR on chromosome 3p25-26)
Vasopressin receptors (V1a/V1b/V2 — low-affinity oxytocin cross-reactivity at supraphysiological concentrations)
receptors: OXTR — Gq/11-coupled class A GPCR; nanomolar affinity for oxytocin
V1a / V1b / V2 vasopressin receptors — low-affinity oxytocin binding at high concentrations

steps: Estrogen upregulates OXTR in myometrium ~200-300-fold by term
Oxytocin binds myometrial OXTR → Gq → PLCβ → IP3 / Ca2+ release + DAG / PKC
Elevated cytosolic Ca2+ drives MLCK-mediated myosin light chain phosphorylation and uterine smooth muscle contraction
Cervical stretch during labor triggers Ferguson reflex: additional hypothalamic oxytocin release in a positive feedback loop
Suckling stimulus activates hypothalamic oxytocin neurons
Oxytocin binds OXTR on mammary myoepithelial cells
Myoepithelial contraction forces milk from alveoli into ducts (milk let-down)
Central oxytocin release (PVN → limbic projections) and peripheral OXTR activation in the brain
Reduced amygdala reactivity to social threat; enhanced prefrontal-amygdala connectivity
Downstream effects on trust, in-group bonding, stress buffering — context-dependent and not uniformly 'prosocial'

expanded.humanEvidence: Synthetic oxytocin (Pitocin) is one of the most widely used medications in obstetrics, with FDA approval for labor induction, labor augmentation, and prevention/treatment of postpartum hemorrhage supported by decades of randomized trials and observational data. It is on the WHO Essential Medicines List. Intranasal oxytocin has been studied extensively in behavioral neuroscience, autism spectrum disorder, PTSD, depression, schizophrenia, and relationship research; findings have been inconsistent and the early 'social panacea' narrative has not replicated in large trials. Intranasal oxytocin is not FDA-approved for any behavioral or psychiatric indication. A commercial Syntocinon nasal spray for lactation support was previously available in the US but has been discontinued. Compounded intranasal oxytocin is available off-label.
expanded.animalEvidence: Foundational animal work established oxytocin's role in parturition (Dale 1906), milk ejection (Ott and Scott), pair bonding (prairie voles), and maternal behavior. Rodent and primate studies elucidated central OXTR distribution and social-behavior effects; zebrafish work (2023) supports a conserved role in social fear responsiveness.
expanded.mechanismDetail: Oxytocin is a cyclic nonapeptide (Cys-Tyr-Ile-Gln-Asn-Cys-Pro-Leu-Gly-NH2; MW 1007; C43H66N12O12S2; CAS 50-56-6) stabilized by a disulfide bridge between Cys1 and Cys6 that forms a 6-residue ring plus a 3-residue tail, with C-terminal amidation catalyzed by PAM (an ascorbate-dependent enzyme). It is synthesized as a pre-prohormone with neurophysin I in hypothalamic magnocellular PVN and SON neurons, cleaved during axonal transport, and released from the posterior pituitary. Signaling is mediated primarily by the 389-residue class A Gq-coupled oxytocin receptor (OXTR; chromosome 3p25-26) → PLCβ → IP3/DAG → intracellular Ca2+ rise → MLCK-driven smooth muscle contraction in uterine and mammary tissue; downstream MAPK and PI3K cascades are also engaged. Myometrial OXTR density rises ~200-300-fold at term under estrogen drive, explaining the increased uterotonic efficacy of Pitocin near labor. Structural similarity to vasopressin (2 amino acid differences) allows cross-reactivity: at high doses oxytocin acts at V2 receptors, producing antidiuretic effects that can cause water intoxication. Plasma half-life is ~1-6 minutes. The blood-brain barrier limits peripheral-to-central transfer; intranasal administration is thought to access the CNS via olfactory/trigeminal pathways, with peak central effects at ~30-45 minutes.
expanded.safetyDetail: IV/IM oxytocin has a well-characterized obstetric safety profile; key risks are uterine tachysystole/hyperstimulation and fetal distress, water intoxication from the antidiuretic effect at high cumulative doses, hypotension with rapid IV boluses, and postpartum hemorrhage if misused. Appropriate monitoring (continuous electronic fetal monitoring, titration protocols) mitigates most of these. Intranasal oxytocin in research and compounded-prescription contexts has a favorable short-term safety profile — mainly nasal irritation, mild drowsiness, and occasional headache — but long-term safety of chronic intranasal use is not established, and chronic administration may affect endogenous vasopressin responsiveness. SSRIs may blunt OXTR sensitivity; chronic alcohol use reduces OXTR expression. Do not combine with other uterotonics without obstetric oversight.

safetyRisks.label: Uterine tachysystole / fetal distress
safetyRisks.helpSummary: Well-titrated IV dosing under monitoring is safe and highly effective
safetyRisks.harmSummary: Excessive doses can cause uterine hyperstimulation, placental abruption, and fetal hypoxia; requires continuous fetal/uterine monitoring

safetyRisks.label: Water intoxication / hyponatremia
safetyRisks.harmSummary: High cumulative IV doses engage V2 receptors and cause antidiuretic effects → dilutional hyponatremia, seizures

safetyRisks.label: Non-uniform 'prosocial' effects
safetyRisks.helpSummary: Intranasal dosing is generally well tolerated short-term
safetyRisks.harmSummary: Oxytocin's central effects are context-dependent: it can enhance in-group bonding while amplifying out-group wariness or jealousy, and psychiatric trial results have been inconsistent

enhancementPotential.summary: Effective and approved for obstetric indications. For claimed 'social / bonding / libido' enhancement in healthy adults, clinical evidence is mixed; early optimistic findings have not robustly replicated and the effects appear context-dependent.
enhancementPotential.caveat: Intranasal oxytocin is not FDA-approved for any behavioral or psychiatric use. Compounded formulations are available by prescription but their purity, dose delivery, and nasal absorption vary.

missingEvidence: Replicated large-scale phase 3 data for autism spectrum disorder, PTSD, and social anxiety
Long-term safety of chronic intranasal use
Pharmacokinetic consensus on intranasal → CNS bioavailability
Head-to-head comparisons with longer-acting OXTR agonists (e.g., carbetocin)
anecdotalUse: Intranasal oxytocin marketed in 'wellness' channels for bonding, sexual intimacy, stress reduction — off-label
Compounded intranasal oxytocin for ASD social-cognition support

claims.value: FDA-approved (Pitocin IV/IM) for labor induction, labor augmentation, and control of postpartum hemorrhage / uterine atony; intranasal oxytocin for behavioral or psychiatric indications is NOT FDA-approved
Cyclic nonapeptide, Cys-Tyr-Ile-Gln-Asn-Cys-Pro-Leu-Gly-NH2, disulfide between Cys1 and Cys6; MW 1007.19; formula C43H66N12O12S2; CAS 50-56-6; C-terminal amidation catalyzed by PAM

Oxytocin receptor (OXTR) is a 389-aa Gq-coupled class A GPCR; activation drives PLCβ → IP3/Ca2+ and DAG/PKC; myometrial OXTR density rises ~200-300-fold at term under estrogen drive. Structural similarity to vasopressin yields low-affinity V1a/V1b/V2 cross-reactivity, including antidiuretic effects at high doses

Intranasal oxytocin has been studied in autism, PTSD, social anxiety, schizophrenia, FTD, and couple therapy; results have been mixed, often null in large trials, and there is no FDA approval for any behavioral or psychiatric use
